feed.title: Arriba Chocolat
feed.subtitle: Le défi d'une vie: goûter à toutes les barres de chocolat faites à partir des meilleures fèves de cacao qui soient: le cacao fino d'aroma d'Équateur.

entry.title: Hacienda San José - 80% Chocolate Negro - Los Rios

link.title: Hacienda San José - 80% Chocolate Negro - Los Rios

entry.content:
Hacienda San José comes from a line of cocoa-producing farms founded in the 1930s, during Ecuador’s cocoa boom. Renowned for its high quality cocoa beans (the best in the world! according to the text on the cover), it wasn’t until 90 years later that the owner family decided to produce its own chocolate.

Butter and hazelnut mingle with the slightly bitter, acidic notes of coffee, finishing with aromas of ripe banana. This is a chocolate that excels in the classic category - no regrets, but no surprises.

Evaluation

Uniqueness: 3.5
Finesse: 4.5
Comfort: 4.5
Intensity: 4.5
General impression: 4

Final evaluation: 21/25
entry.summary: Hacienda San José comes from a line of cocoa-producing farms founded in the 1930s, during Ecuador’s cocoa boom. Renowned for its high quality cocoa beans (the best in the world! according to the text on the cover), it wasn’t until 90 years later that the owner family decided to produce its own chocolate.

entry.title: Anquria - 63% El Cacao Blanco - Macambo Caramelizado

link.title: Anquria - 63% El Cacao Blanco - Macambo Caramelizado

entry.content:
By now, you’re familiar with macambo — a cousin of cocoa that can be made into a chocolate equivalent (not as good), but can also be added in pieces to a bar, much like a nut. Here, Anquria — a new brand for me — caramelizes it beforehand, adding a touch of praline that I won’t complain about.

The chocolate itself is hard to isolate, but it leans toward the herbal and spicy: tobacco, clove, mushroom. Combined with the macambo, the result is unusual — and quietly compelling.

Evaluation

Uniqueness: 4.5
Finesse: 4.5
Comfort: 4
Intensity: 4.5
General impression: 4

Final evaluation: 21.5/25
entry.summary: By now, you’re familiar with macambo — a cousin of cocoa that can be made into a chocolate equivalent (not as good), but can also be added in pieces to a bar, much like a nut. Here, Anquria — a new brand for me — caramelizes it beforehand, adding a touch of praline that I won’t complain about.

entry.title: Wao - 70% - Cacao del Yasuni
entry.content:
Again, a chocolate from Yasuni - here, a project run by women of the Waorani community, one of the many peoples of this region of the Amazon.

This is a chocolate with an assumed power, a little raw, which begins with notes of plum liqueur and freshly cut wood, with a finish of butter and brownie. The texture, perfectly smooth, crunchy then melting, is well mastered.

An excellent chocolate.

Evaluation

Uniqueness: 4.5
Finesse: 5
Comfort: 4.5
Intensity: 4.5
General impression: 4.5

Final evaluation: 23/25
entry.summary: Again, a chocolate from Yasuni - here, a project run by women of the Waorani community, one of the many peoples of this region of the Amazon.

entry.title: Avanaa - 70% - Zorza Dominican Republic
entry.content:
Avanaa offers us a chocolate of great intensity. Right from the start, it unfurls strong notes of wild berries, sandalwood and even red wine. Although the cocoa comes from the Dominican Republic, I immediately thought of Alluvia’s Vietnamese chocolate - they’re in the same range.

A successful chocolate from a Montreal bean-to-bar producer.

Evaluation

Uniqueness: 4.5
Finesse: 4.5
Comfort: 4
Intensity: 5
General impression: 4.5

Final evaluation: 22.5/25
entry.summary: Avanaa offers us a chocolate of great intensity. Right from the start, it unfurls strong notes of wild berries, sandalwood and even red wine. Although the cocoa comes from the Dominican Republic, I immediately thought of Alluvia’s Vietnamese chocolate - they’re in the same range.

entry.title: Belly’s Cocoa - Chocolate 62% Cacao - Sabor Café

link.title: Belly’s Cocoa - Chocolate 62% Cacao - Sabor Café

entry.content:
Belly’s chocolate is always special - as much for its granular texture as for its unique, light taste, somewhere between citrus, flower and sweet butter. Here, we add a few notes of coffee. It’s present, but subtle: it adds color to the chocolate without taking control of it. For me, who’s not a fan of coffee, it’s a success: I appreciate the notes without being overwhelmed, just as I can appreciate them in a tiramisu. An interesting variation.

Evaluation

Uniqueness: 5
Finesse: 5
Comfort: 4
Intensity: 4.5
General impression: 4.5

Final evaluation: 23/25
entry.summary: Belly’s chocolate is always special - as much for its granular texture as for its unique, light taste, somewhere between citrus, flower and sweet butter. Here, we add a few notes of coffee. It’s present, but subtle: it adds color to the chocolate without taking control of it. For me, who’s not a fan of coffee, it’s a success: I appreciate the notes without being overwhelmed, just as I can appreciate them in a tiramisu. An interesting variation.

entry.title: Leyenda - Dark Chocolate 80%
entry.content:
Maybe it’s because it’s March 22, and Montreal has just had another 10 centimetres of snow. But in any case, what strikes me about this tablet are its floral notes. They transport me to the botanical gardens, in two months’ time, taking photo after photo of the lilacs and rhododendrons, amazed as I am every year by the return of colors, sounds and fragrances.

This is a chocolate that focuses on the quality of its cocoa, bringing out its finesse and originality.

Evaluation

Uniqueness: 4.5
Finesse: 4.5
Comfort: 5
Intensity: 4.5
General impression: 4.5

Final evaluation: 23/25
entry.summary: Maybe it’s because it’s March 22, and Montreal has just had another 10 centimetres of snow. But in any case, what strikes me about this tablet are its floral notes. They transport me to the botanical gardens, in two months’ time, taking photo after photo of the lilacs and rhododendrons, amazed as I am every year by the return of colors, sounds and fragrances.

entry.title: Victory 60% Pino
entry.content:
The pine essence plays a major role in this bar. Refreshing, well-sweetened and silky, it’s a pleasant chocolate, but not particularly striking.

Evaluation

Uniqueness: 4
Finesse: 4
Comfort: 4
Intensity: 4
General impression: 4

Final evaluation: 20/25
entry.summary: The pine essence plays a major role in this bar. Refreshing, well-sweetened and silky, it’s a pleasant chocolate, but not particularly striking.

entry.title: Akao 70% - Mandarina, Naranja & Curcuma
entry.content:
Pieces of candied orange and mandarin peel and turmeric sprinkled under the bar mingle with dark, intense chocolate in a subtle, sunny dance. This is a great discovery from Akao, which seems to specialize in high-quality flavor blends.

Evaluation

Uniqueness: 5
Finesse: 4.5
Comfort: 4.5
Intensity: 5
General impression: 4.5

Final evaluation: 23.5/25
entry.summary: Pieces of candied orange and mandarin peel and turmeric sprinkled under the bar mingle with dark, intense chocolate in a subtle, sunny dance. This is a great discovery from Akao, which seems to specialize in high-quality flavor blends.

entry.title: Huma 70% - Manabi
entry.content:
This isn’t the first Huma chocolate to be strongly reminiscent of pear and almond. This one, however, is not only fruity, but also bears the taste characteristics of strong cocoa fermentation and raw cane sugar.

It’s a unique, intense chocolate with a clear signature. A delight from Huma.

Evaluation

Uniqueness: 5
Finesse: 4.5
Comfort: 4.5
Intensity: 5
General impression: 5

Final evaluation: 24/25
entry.summary: This isn’t the first Huma chocolate to be strongly reminiscent of pear and almond. This one, however, is not only fruity, but also bears the taste characteristics of strong cocoa fermentation and raw cane sugar.

entry.title: Rights - Chocolate with a Purpose - 70% Aji

link.title: Rights - Chocolate with a Purpose - 70% Aji

entry.content:
Rights is a brand whose mission — and marketing strategy — is to employ people with disabilities. Since my last experience with them, the packaging has evolved: from festive colors toward a more refined aesthetic.

The chocolate itself hasn’t changed. Woody and elegant, it is lightly sweetened with panela. The chili — ají — is not very strong, remaining discreet in the background while bringing a pleasant warming touch.

Evaluation

Originality: 4
Fineness: 4
Comforting: 5
Intensity: 4.5
Overall impression: 4.5

Final evaluation: 22/25
entry.summary: Rights is a brand whose mission — and marketing strategy — is to employ people with disabilities. Since my last experience with them, the packaging has evolved: from festive colors toward a more refined aesthetic.
